coredata.dc:title: Regulation of particle size and surface state to realize multicolor solid carbon dots for white light emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zeyu
Yao, Qing
Shi, Jindou
Da, Zheyuan
Wang, Junnan
Allam, Ahmed
Alfassam, Haifa E.
Li, Xiangming
Shao, Jinyou
Wang, Minqiang
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor solid-state carbon dots (MSCDs) with superior photothermal stability and tunable fluorescence exhibit significant potential for applications in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and optoelectronic devices. However, the development of a facile and efficient synthetic method using simple raw materials remains a challenge. In this study, blue (B), green (G), and red (R) emissive carbon dots (B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) are synthesized by varying pre-treatment methods and raw material ratios, using 1-(1-Naphthyl)ethylamine (1-NEA) as the carbon source and boric acid (BA) as the matrix. B-CDs, prepared via freeze-drying pre-treatment in a nitrogen (N2) protective atmosphere, exhibits the smallest particle size and the largest emission bandgap. Conversely, G-CDs and R-CDs display larger particle sizes, with R-CDs having the smallest emission bandgap due to differing raw material ratios that introduced distinct surface state defects. These variations in emission bandgaps result in diverse fluorescence emissions under ultraviolet (UV) light excitation. Leveraging the excellent luminescent properties, white LEDs (W-LEDs) are fabricated by combining and coating the CDs onto a 400 nm LED chip. The resulting W-LEDs demonstrate outstanding photothermal stability and a high color rendering index (CRI) of 90–93 during prolonged operation. This work provides valuable insights for the application of MSCDs in W-LED technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor
Solid-state carbon dots
Light emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of multicolor and white LEDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFB2011500 and 2022YFE0122500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 52161145103 and 61774124), and 111 Program (No. B14040), and Shaanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program (No.2021GXLH-Z-084). Also, the authors acknowledge Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2025R400), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for synthesizing B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs.
TEM images of MSCDs, a,d) average size of the CDs in B-CDs is 4.0 nm; b,e) average size of the CDs in G-CDs is 15.8 nm; c,f) average size of the CDs in R-CDs is 16.3 nm, inset are HRTEM images.

Although element components for B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs are highly similar, bound portions among them are significantly different for incusing emission property variation; a) XRD patterns indicate three CDs sharing similar B2O3 matrix skeletons with varied density; b) FT-IR spectra indicate the matrix of B-CDs has highest density by the strongest transmittance of bounds; c) for XPS spectra indicate three CDs sharing similar element components; d) portions of C–N/C–O of B-CDs and R-CDs are higher than G-CDs due to the higher abundance of BA added; e) introduction of oxygen during pyrolysis increase C–O portions for G-CDs and R-CDs; f) bond modifications derive absorption spectra difference by adjusting sp2 conjugation domains for influence emission of the CDs by π-bond conjugation.

Emission spectrum of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs exhibit high PL consistency; a, b, c) actual pictures of MSCDs under natural and UV light; d, e, f) fluorescence spectra at different excitations of the MSCDs; g, h, i) time-resolved PL spectra indicate outstand PL consistency among different color CDs.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence emission mechanism of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs.

Color luminance and stability performances of LEDs fabricated by the synthesized MCCDs; a-d) emission spectra of B-LED, G-LED, R-LED, and W-LED; e) actual pictures luminated by the fabricated LEDs; f) CIE coordinates indicate the colored LEDs exhibit bright blue, green and red fluorescence and can generate natural white light; g) 300 min continuous operation of W-LED only derive slight temperature increase; h) the luminance intensity and CCT drop slightly during long-term operation.

head.ce:title: Regulation of particle size and surface state to realize multicolor solid carbon dots for white light emitting diodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor solid-state carbon dots (MSCDs) with superior photothermal stability and tunable fluorescence exhibit significant potential for applications in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and optoelectronic devices. However, the development of a facile and efficient synthetic method using simple raw materials remains a challenge. In this study, blue (B), green (G), and red (R) emissive carbon dots (B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) are synthesized by varying pre-treatment methods and raw material ratios, using 1-(1-Naphthyl)ethylamine (1-NEA) as the carbon source and boric acid (BA) as the matrix. B-CDs, prepared via freeze-drying pre-treatment in a nitrogen (N2) protective atmosphere, exhibits the smallest particle size and the largest emission bandgap. Conversely, G-CDs and R-CDs display larger particle sizes, with R-CDs having the smallest emission bandgap due to differing raw material ratios that introduced distinct surface state defects. These variations in emission bandgaps result in diverse fluorescence emissions under ultraviolet (UV) light excitation. Leveraging the excellent luminescent properties, white LEDs (W-LEDs) are fabricated by combining and coating the CDs onto a 400 nm LED chip. The resulting W-LEDs demonstrate outstanding photothermal stability and a high color rendering index (CRI) of 90–93 during prolonged operation. This work provides valuable insights for the application of MSCDs in W-LED technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging class of photoluminescent (PL) materials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered widespread attention for applications in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, photoelectrocatalysis and sensing due to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable luminescent properties, and unique physicochemical properties [1–5]. Significant advancements have been made in designing and synthesizing CDs with efficient fluorescence emission in solution [6–8]. However, achieving photoluminescence (PL) emission from solid-state CDs (SCDs) remains challenging due to excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π–π interactions, similar to those observed in organic molecules [9,10]. Specifically, when the distance (D) between adjacent CDs becomes sufficiently small (D < R0, where R0 is the Förster distance) excessive Förster resonance energy transfer occurs, leading to self-quenching [11]. To mitigate this issue, steric hindrance is often introduced between CD particles. A practical approach involves embedding CDs in an inert solid matrix, such as polymers [12], zeolite [13], inorganic salts [14], or oxides [15]. Light-emitting diodes (LEDs), as semiconductor devices, directly convert electrical energy into light and offer numerous advantages over traditional light sources, including energy saving, environment friendliness, high luminous efficacy, safety, long lifespan, compact size, and high reliability [16,17]. While SCDs are frequently employed in LED applications, single-color SCDs are often insufficient for achieving white light emission in white LED (W-LED). Consequently, the development of multicolor SCDs (MSCDs) represents a significant and meaningful area of research.

The fluorescence mechanism of multicolor CDs (MCDs) is influenced by various factors, with the most widely accepted mechanisms among researchers being the size effect, surface state, molecular state, and cross-linked enhanced emission (CEE) effect, which are also the most frequently reported [18–20]. The quantum confinement effect in CDs causes the separation of energy levels, where the discrete energy level spacing is significantly influenced by the size and structure of the CDs. The relationship between tunable fluorescence and the particle size of multicolor solid-state CDs (MSCDs) has been demonstrated in numerous studies [21–23]. Ding et al. synthesized MCDs from l-glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine using a solvothermal method in four different solvents (formamide, dimethylformamide, ethanol, and aqueous H2SO4 solution) [24]. he distinct optical properties of these CDs were attributed to differences in particle size, graphitic nitrogen content, and oxygen-containing functional groups, which were modulated by controlling the dehydration and carbonization processes during solvothermal reactions. Although this approach yielded bright MCDs, the hydrothermal reaction and the use of various organic solvents limit its practical applicability. Additionally, the influence of surface states on the photoluminescence (PL) of CDs arises primarily from surface defects and functional groups. Surface defects, often caused by oxidation, act as exciton trapping centers, resulting in fluorescence emissions [25]. The formation of multiple emission domains, influenced by varying degrees of surface oxidation, has been observed, with higher oxidation levels generally enhancing emission efficiency [26]. Surface functional groups also play a critical role in creating distinct emission domains, contributing to the multicolor emissions of MCDs. By manipulating reaction parameters such as solvent [27,28], temperature [29,30], and the type or proportion of precursors [31–33], MCDs with diverse surface states can be synthesized. Liu et al. produced three types of CDs exhibiting blue, green, and orange photoluminescence using microwave heating, and applied them in bioimaging and tunable color temperature W-LEDs [34]. In this case, the multiple emissions were attributed to the degree of surface oxidation and the abundance of surface N-functional groups. However, this approach, while simple, lacked the use of a single carbon source and failed to produce red-emitting CDs, reducing its competitiveness for practical applications. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop a straightforward method for synthesizing MSCDs using a simple carbon source and facile preparation process.

In this work, blue (B), green (G), and red (R) emissive carbon dots (B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs) were synthesized from 1-(1-Naphthyl)ethylamine (1-NEA) and boric acid (BA) via high-temperature pyrolysis. The fluorescence emissions of the CDs were tuned by varying the synthesis conditions and the ratios of raw materials. The freeze-drying pretreatment combined with synthesis in a nitrogen (N2) protective atmosphere resulted in a tightly structured B2O3 matrix, restricting the growth of B-CDs and leading to the smallest particle sizes. The smaller particle size corresponds to a wider emission bandgap, enabling the B-CDs to exhibit bright blue luminescence under UV excitation. In contrast, G-CDs and R-CDs, synthesized without freeze-drying and under atmospheric air, experienced unrestricted growth. For R-CDs, the high BA ratio in the precursor mixture introduced more surface state defects, resulting in the smallest emission bandgap. Thus, multicolor solid-state CDs (MSCDs) with blue, green, and red emissions were successfully obtained by controlling the particle size and surface state defects of the CDs. Notably, the photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of the MSCDs synthesized in this study are achieved through a comprehensive coupling of factors, including CDs particle size, bonding interactions between the B2O3 matrix and CDs, and surface chemical modifications of the CDs, while maintaining advanced photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY). Specifically, the average emission lifetimes for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are 10.39 ns, 8.97 ns, and 5.89 ns, respectively, with corresponding PLQY values of 46.86 %, 43.22 %, and 31.38 %, respectively, indicating the high consistency and advanced PL properties of the MSCDs synthesized using the reported approach [35,36]. Benefiting from this high consistency, monochromatic LEDs fabricated using individual MSCD types exhibit excellent power tolerance and a linear increase in luminance intensity with increasing power, a critical requirement for adjustable, high-power commercial lighting systems. Furthermore, by optimizing the ratio of MSCDs, a high-performance white LED (W-LED) was produced, capable of emitting natural-like white light. At a drive current of 80 mA, the W-LED achieved a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4920 K and a color rendering index (CRI) of 92, surpassing the performance of most CDs-based W-LEDs [37–39].

Briefly, B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing 1-NEA and BA as raw materials at 200 °C for 3 h. By altering the synthesis conditions and the ratios of raw materials (Fig. 1 ), CDs with varying sizes and surface states were obtained, enabling modulation of the emission bandgap and achieving multicolor emissions of MSCDs. The freeze-drying pretreatment of B-CDs, combined with the use of an N2 protective atmosphere during high-temperature pyrolysis, resulted in smaller particle sizes and the largest bandgap among the samples. Consequently, B-CDs exhibited bright blue luminescence under UV excitation. In contrast, G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized by further tuning the size and surface state of the CDs, facilitating the generation of green and red luminescence, respectively.

The absence of freeze-drying pretreatment and the synthesis of G-CDs and R-CDs in an air atmosphere resulted in the generation of NH3 gas from 1-NEA at high temperatures, leading to the formation of numerous pores on the surfaces of G-CDs and R-CDs. In the TEM image (Fig. 2 ), it is evident that the CDs are uniformly embedded within the B2O3 matrix framework. Importantly, the B2O3 matrix serves as an inert solid matrix for the CDs, preventing aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and providing the foundational conditions necessary for the fluorescence emission of solid-state CDs (SCDs). The average particle size of B-CDs was measured at 4.0 nm (Fig. 2d), a result attributed to the freeze-drying pretreatment and high-temperature pyrolysis conducted under an N2 protective atmosphere, which facilitated the formation of a densely structured B2O3 matrix. This restricted growth led to the smallest particle size among the samples. In contrast, the average particle sizes of G-CDs and R-CDs were 15.8 nm (Figs. 2e) and 16.3 nm (Fig. 2f), respectively, significantly larger than those of B-CDs. This size increase was associated with the substantial NH3 gas evolution during their synthesis, resulting in a more porous structure in the B2O3 matrix. The lattice stripe spacing is 0.19 nm of CDs, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite. SEM images further corroborate these findings, showing that B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs exhibit irregular block-like structures (Fig. S1).

In addition to determining the matrix structure, differences in the abundance of synthesis-derived covalent bonds are critical for influencing the emission spectrum by regulating the bonding strength between the CDs and the matrix, which affects the number of sp2 conjugation domains and variations in the degree of π-bond conjugation. The XRD pattern exhibited three distinct diffraction peaks at 15.1°, 28.3°, and 40.1° (Fig. 3 a), corresponding to the diffraction peaks of B2O3 [40], indicating that intramolecular hydrogen bonds in BA molecules were disrupted at high temperatures, leading to dehydration and condensation processes that formed B2O3 [41], Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed to further investigate the functional groups and chemical composition of the samples. The FT-IR spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs showed characteristic absorption peaks at 3250, 1455, and 1192 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H, B–O, and B–C, respectively [42,43], respectively (Fig. 3b). The FT-IR spectral peaks of B-CDs exhibited higher intensity, reflecting the denser matrix structure of B-CDs, which leads to stronger chemical bonding between the CDs and the B2O3 matrix. The XPS spectra (Fig. 3c) confirmed the presence of B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s elements in B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, with the corresponding elemental contents listed in Table S1. The B 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 194.19, 193.51, and 192.57 eV, corresponding to BCO2, B2O3, and B–O, respectively (Fig. S2a), indicating the formation of covalently crosslinked boronic anhydride (mixed anhydride) structures [44,45]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) revealed peaks at 284.7 and 286.6 eV, attributed to C–C/CC and C–N/C–O bonds, respectively, with the chemical bond contents shown in Table S2. The C–N/C–O content of B-CDs and R-CDs was higher than that of G-CDs, likely due to the higher BA ratio in the raw materials of B-CDs and R-CDs, resulting in more CDs forming C–O–B bonds with the B2O3 matrix. The N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2b) displayed two peaks at 401 and 402.1 eV, corresponding to N–H and C–N bonds, respectively, with B-CDs showing a higher N elemental content due to high-temperature pyrolysis under N2 (Table S1). Additionally, the C–N content of B-CDs was higher than that of G-CDs and R-CDs (Table S3), indicating that the C–N bond contributes most to surface state defects in B-CDs, whereas the C–O bond dominates surface state defects in G-CDs and R-CDs. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) revealed peaks at 532.5 and 533.4 eV, attributed to B–O and C–O bonds, respectively, with the C–O bond content following the trend B-CDs < G-CDs < R-CDs (Table S4), indicating that variations in surface state oxygen content strongly influence the fluorescence emission properties of the CDs. The UV–visible absorption spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs (Fig. 3f) exhibit distinct features. For B-CDs, two prominent broad peaks are observed at 240 nm and 365 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transitions of the CC/CN bonds in the carbon core and the n-π∗ transitions of the C–N/CN or C–O/CO bonds on the carbon dot surface, respectively [24,46]. In contrast, the absorption peaks for G-CDs and R-CDs are red-shifted, appearing at 300 nm and 410 nm for π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions, respectively. This significant red-shift is attributed to the larger particle sizes and increased sp2 conjugation domains of G-CDs and R-CDs, which enhance the degree of π-bond conjugation. As a result, the energy required for the π-π∗ transition is lower, leading to the observed red-shift in their UV–visible absorption spectra.

The successful regulation of the crystalline sp2 carbon core conjugation domains and the bonding between CDs and the matrix enabled the synthesis of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs with distinct fluorescence emissions. Photographs of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs under natural and UV light display blue, green, and red luminescence, respectively (Fig. 4 a, b, and c). Notably, the emission spectrum of B-CDs shows two distinct fluorescence peaks at 420 nm and 450 nm, with increasing excitation wavelength attenuating the 420 nm emission and enhancing the 450 nm emission (Fig. 4d, e, and f). According to XPS results, the dominant surface state defects of B-CDs involve C–N and C–O bonds, with the 420 nm and 450 nm emission peaks attributed to vibrational electron transitions of these bonds, corresponding to excitations at approximately 370 nm and 410 nm, respectively. In contrast, the emission spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs are centered at 520 nm and 590/635 nm, respectively, exhibiting minimal shifts with changes in excitation wavelength (Fig. 4e and f). This stability aligns with the dominance of C–O bonds in the surface states of G-CDs and R-CDs compared to B-CDs. Differences in bonds and sp2 domains also influence photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) and lifetimes, as revealed by time-resolved PL decay curves. The average lifetimes were 10.39 ns, 8.97 ns, and 5.89 ns for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively (Fig. 4g, h, and i). These curves were fitted using a dual exponential formula, comprising a short-lived component (τ1) and a long-lived component (τ2), which correspond to recombination processes in the core and surface states, respectively [47]. Although the lifetimes exhibit an approximately twofold difference between B-CDs and R-CDs, the absolute difference of 4.50 ns is significantly smaller than the PL redshift-induced lifetime changes reported in previous studies [35,36]. Furthermore, the PLQY values of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were 46.86 %, 43.22 %, and 31.38 %, respectively (Table S5), demonstrating the advanced consistency among these materials. The fluorescence emission -spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs synthesized at different temperatures (180 °C, 200 °C, 220 °C, and 240 °C) and heating durations (2 h, 2.5 h, 3 h, and 3.5 h) (Figs. S3, S4, and S5) indicate that 200 °C and 3 h are optimal synthesis conditions.

Since MSCDs with blue, green, and red fluorescence emissions can be synthesized by varying pretreatment conditions and raw material ratios, understanding the mechanism behind their tunable fluorescence is essential. As previously noted, most CDs lose their superior photoluminescence (PL) in the solid state due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which occurs when the distance between particles falls below a critical threshold. The introduction of external matrices can physically prevent CD aggregation and block non-radiative- energy transfer pathways [48]. Previous studies have shown that the B2O3 matrix effectively serves as a dispersion medium for CDs, suppressing non-radiative transitions and enabling PL emission for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs [49,50]. Notably, the B2O3 matrix does not alter the electronic energy level distribution of CDs but primarily provides spatial restriction. While achieving fluorescence emission in solid-state CDs (SCDs) is fundamental, the tunability of fluorescence is the key focus for MSCDs. A defining characteristic of CDs is their tendency to exhibit quantum confinement effects, which occur when the CD size is smaller than its exciton Bohr radius [51]. It is well established that the PL of CDs can be modulated by adjusting the size of their conjugated π-domains, with smaller particles exhibiting a blue-shifted emission frequency due to higher energy transitions, as predicted by molecular orbital theory [52]. In this study, B-CDs were pretreated via freeze-drying and pyrolyzed at high temperatures under an N2 atmosphere, resulting in a dense matrix structure that restricted CD growth and produced the smallest particle size. In contrast, G-CDs and R-CDs were not subjected to freeze-drying, leading to significant NH3 gas generation during high-temperature pyrolysis, which created a fluffy matrix structure and larger particle sizes for G-CDs and R-CDs compared to B-CDs. Consequently, B-CDs exhibited the smallest conjugated π-domain and the largest emission bandgap (Fig. 5 ), displaying blue PL emission under UV excitation. Furthermore, the chemical structure of CDs primarily comprises a crystalline sp2 carbon core surrounded by an amorphous polymer shell, typically rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. These complex surface states result in different excitation energy levels, providing CDs with multiple emission centers [53]. In this work, by adjusting the BA ratio in the raw materials for G-CDs and R-CDs, the B2O3 matrix content in R-CDs was significantly higher than in G-CDs. A higher B2O3 matrix content facilitated the formation of more C–O–B bonds, as confirmed by XPS analysis, which revealed a significantly greater C–O content in R-CDs compared to G-CDs. According to previous studies [54–56], surface oxidation defects act as exciton trapping centers, leading to surface state-associated fluorescence emission. Additionally, the strong electronegativity of oxygen atoms in oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., C–O) imparts electron-accepting properties, reducing the LUMO energy level by withdrawing electrons from the π-conjugated skeletons [57]. These distinct surface states (C–O) in G-CDs and R-CDs result in R-CDs having the smallest bandgap. Consequently, G-CDs and R-CDs exhibit green and red PL emissions, respectively, under UV light excitation.

Benefiting from the advanced photoluminescence (PL) consistency, the CDs synthesized in this study demonstrate stable performance on LED platforms. Monochromatic LEDs fabricated using individual types of CDs exhibit emission spectra under 400 nm UV chip excitation that closely match the spectra of the corresponding powder form materials (Fig. 6 a, b, and c). A white LED (W-LED) was assembled using B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs in a mass ratio of 0.9:1.1:1.4, producing white light that closely resembles natural sunlight (Fig. 6d). When used as a light source, the W-LED effectively illuminated and displayed the characters "Xi'an Jiaotong University" and a multi-color five-pointed star, with the high color rendering index (CRI) allowing for accurate color representation (Fig. 6e). Specifically, the CIE coordinates of the B-LED, G-LED, and R-LED are (0.13, 0.13), (0.28, 0.46), and (0.62, 0.36), respectively, indicating bright blue, green, and red fluorescence emissions. The W-LED's CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.32) confirm that the color temperature of the emitted white light closely matches natural light (Fig. 6f). In terms of intensity flexibility, the luminescence intensity of the LED devices assembled with B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs increases progressively with the driving current, without any significant saturation, enabling the W-LED to show a corresponding gradual increase in intensity as the driving current is raised from 1 mA to 80 mA (Figs. S8 and S9). This high power tolerance and linear increase in luminance intensity with power input indicate the suitability of the LEDs for adjustable high-power commercial lighting systems. Notably, at a driving current of 80 mA, the W-LED achieves a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4920 K and a CRI of 92, outperforming most CDs-based W-LEDs reported in the literature [37–39].

Facilitated by the high temperatures during synthesis, the fabricated LEDs exhibit remarkable thermal stability, overcoming a common challenge faced by many photoluminescent (PL) materials [58,59]. The continuous luminance performance of the W-LED was confirmed to be stable during a 300-min operation at 80 mA, with only a modest temperature increase from 299.7 K to 332.7 K (Fig. 6g). High-temperature spectral variation tests of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs showed that the PL intensity of the MSCDs decreased slightly when heated from room temperature (298 K) to 423 K, while retaining strong luminescence characteristics suitable for LED device applications (Fig. S6). Additionally, the stability of the MSCDs, evaluated through prolonged conventional ambient storage, demonstrated excellent photostability, with the LEDs maintaining reliable performance after 90 days (Fig. S7). Notably, over this long-term operation, the luminous efficiency decreased slightly from an initial 420 cd/m2 to 385 cd/m2, and the correlated color temperature (CCT) declined from 4920 K to 4492 K, corresponding to retention rates of 91.7 % and 91.3 %, respectively (Fig. 6h). Furthermore, the relationship between runtime and CRI (color rendering index) for the W-LED device was investigated (Fig. S10), showing that the CRI remained virtually unchanged with extended runtime. In summary, W-LED devices prepared by mixing B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited consistent and reliable performance under prolonged operation, significantly enhancing the potential of MSCDs for future applications in the W-LED device market.

In summary, B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs were successfully synthesized via high-temperature pyrolysis of 1-NEA and BA, with the B2O3 matrix providing an excellent framework to prevent aggregation-caused self-quenching of CDs. The B2O3 matrix in B-CDs was structurally dense due to freeze-drying pre-processing and synthesis under an N2 atmosphere, resulting in the smallest particle size and the largest emission bandgap. In contrast, G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized without freeze-drying and pyrolyzed in an air environment, generating significant NH3 gas during the process. Consequently, the B2O3 matrix in these samples developed a fluffy structure, leading to much larger particle sizes for G-CDs and R-CDs compared to B-CDs and causing a pronounced redshift in photoluminescence (PL) emission. Furthermore, the higher BA ratio in the raw materials for R-CDs compared to G-CDs resulted in a greater number of C–O–B bonds, significantly increasing the C–O bond content in the surface state defects of R-CDs, which contributed to their smallest bandgap. Under 400 nm UV excitation, B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited blue, green, and red luminescence, respectively. With advanced PLQY consistency, a white LED (W-LED) with CIE coordinates of (0.32, 0.32) was fabricated by combining B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs. The W-LED exhibits a linear relationship between luminance intensity and power, along with excellent thermal and optical stability during prolonged operation, establishing the synthesized CDs as a promising material for future LED device applications.
1-(1-Naphthyl)ethylamine (1-NEA) was obtained from Macklin Co. Ltd. Boric acid (BA) was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Deionzed water was got from a milipore purification system.

B-CDs were synthesized in the following method: first 10 μL of 1-NEA and 0.4 g of BA were added to 10 ml pure water and stirred for 30 min followed by freeze-drying for 24 h, and then held at 200 °C for 3 h under the protection of nitrogen atmosphere. For G-CDs, 10 μL of 1-NEA and 0.2 g of BA were first added to 5 ml of pure water and stirred for 30 min. The beaker was covered with tin foil to avoid rapid evaporation of water, and then heated at 200°Cfor 3 h. The synthesis of R-CDs was similar to the G-CDs, with the only difference being that the raw materials were 10 μL of 1-NEA and 1.0 g of BA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of multicolor and white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Commercially available UV chips (400 nm) with an operating voltage of 3.2 V were used as an excitation source for fabricating all types of LEDs. Specifically, 0.1 g of the prepared samples (B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs) were mixed with 1 ml of UV-curable glue. Subsequently, the mixed colloid was stirred well and coated on the surface of the UV-LED chip, and finally irradiated under UV light for 1 min to obtain reusable LEDs. Similarly, mixing B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs in the ratio of 0.9:1.1:1.4 according to this method can obtain W-LEDs with excellent performance.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using the DB-ADVANCE X-ray diffraction analyzer diffractometer. The morphology of samples were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quatan FEG 250). The microstructure of samples was performed on a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the samples was measured using a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi and the surface functional groups were investigated using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) on a Thermofisher Nicolet iS10 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and time-resolved PL spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS 1000 spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance spectra were recorded by PE Lambda 950. The EL spectra of LED were collected by a Keithley 2400 sourcemeter and a Photo Research 670 spectrometer. The thermal infrared imager was used to detect temperature and record thermal images (FOTRIC, USA).

Zeyu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qing Yao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jindou Shi: Methodology, Investigation. Zheyuan Da: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Junnan Wang: Methodology. Ahmed Allam: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Haifa E. Alfassam: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Xiangming Li: Methodology, Validation. Jinyou Shao: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Minqiang Wang: Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2021YFB2011500 and 2022YFE0122500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 52161145103 and 61774124), and 111 Program (No. B14040), and Shaanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program (No.2021GXLH-Z-084). Also, the authors acknowledge Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2025R400), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2025.102608.
